Back: Application of statistical methods to economic data. Formula: Econometrics = Economic Theory + Data + Statistics

Front: What is the error term u and what does it contain?
Back: Collects everything not in the model: omitted variables, unobservable factors, measurement error, random shocks, functional form imperfections

Front: Correlation vs causation — why can't you infer causation from correlation?
Back: Two variables can correlate because: A causes B, B causes A, both caused by C, or it's accidental. Need ceteris paribus + controlled variation to establish causation
Holding all other relevant factors fixed. The core logic of regression — isolating the effect of one variable while controlling for others
Observations on many different units at a single point in time. Example: wages of workers surveyed in 2024
Observations on one or more variables over time. Example: GDP quarterly 2000–2024. Time order matters, serial correlation is a concern
Different units sampled at multiple time periods, then stacked. Units are NOT the same across periods. Example: household surveys from 1985 and 1990
Same units observed across multiple time periods. Advantage: controls for unobserved time-invariant differences. Example: same firms tracked quarterly

Front: Panel vs Pooled cross-section — key difference?
Back: Panel = SAME units over time. Pooled cross-section = DIFFERENT units each period, stacked together
Every population member has equal probability of being selected
Select every k-th unit from an ordered list
Split population into subgroups (strata), then randomly sample within each group. Ensures all subgroups are represented
Randomly select entire groups (clusters); everyone inside is included. Differs from stratified: you sample groups, not within groups

Front: Stratified vs Cluster sampling — key difference?
Back: Stratified: groups predefined, sample WITHIN each group. Cluster: randomly select entire groups, include everyone inside

Front: OLS formula for slope in simple regression
Back: β̂₁ = Σ(xᵢ - x̄)(yᵢ - ȳ) / Σ(xᵢ - x̄)²

Front: OLS formula for intercept in simple regression
Back: β̂₀ = ȳ - β̂₁x̄

Front: When does OLS fail to compute?
Back: When there is no variation in X: Σ(xᵢ - x̄)² = 0. Also fails with perfect multicollinearity (X'X not invertible)

Front: Matrix formula for OLS in multiple regression
Back: β̂ = (X'X)⁻¹X'y
E(u|X) = 0. The average error does not depend on X. Critical for OLS to recover the ceteris paribus effect
R² = 1 - SSR/SST. Fraction of sample variation in y explained by the model. Does NOT validate causality

Front: Adjusted R² — why use it over R²?
Back: R² = 1 - [SSR/(n-k-1)] / [SST/(n-1)]. Penalizes unnecessary variables. R² never falls when adding regressors; adjusted R² can fall

Front: SST = SSE + SSR — what does each term mean?
Back: SST = total variation in y. SSE = explained variation. SSR = unexplained variation (sum of squared residuals)
y = β₀ + β₁x + u. One unit increase in x → β₁ unit change in y
log(y) = β₀ + β₁x + u. One unit increase in x → approximately 100β₁% change in y
y = β₀ + β₁log(x) + u. 1% increase in x → β₁/100 unit change in y
log(y) = β₀ + β₁log(x) + u. β₁ is an elasticity: 1% increase in x → β₁% change in y

Front: If dependent variable is multiplied by constant c, what happens to coefficients?
Back: Intercept and slope both multiplied by c

Front: If independent variable is multiplied by constant c, what happens to its coefficient?
Back: The slope is divided by c. Intercept unchanged

Front: A1: Linearity in parameters — what does violation cause?
Back: Model is misspecified. OLS estimates wrong functional form. Cannot apply standard OLS inference

Front: A2: Random sampling — what does violation cause?
Back: Serial correlation: error terms across observations are correlated. Standard errors are wrong, inference invalid

Front: A3: No perfect multicollinearity — what does violation cause?
Back: OLS cannot be computed. X'X is not invertible. Fix: drop one of the perfectly collinear variables

Front: A4: Zero conditional mean — what does violation cause?
Back: OLS coefficients are BIASED. Called endogeneity. Most common cause: omitted variable correlated with regressors

Front: A5: Homoskedasticity — what does violation cause?
Back: Coefficients remain UNBIASED. But standard errors are wrong → t-tests and p-values are invalid. Fix: robust standard errors

Front: A6: Normality of errors — what does violation cause?
Back: t and F tests invalid in small samples. In large samples, CLT saves you — normality less critical

Front: Gauss-Markov theorem — what does it state?
Back: Under A1-A5, OLS is BLUE: Best Linear Unbiased Estimator. Best = minimum variance among all linear unbiased estimators
Var(β̂ⱼ) = σ² / [SSTⱼ(1 - Rⱼ²)]. Larger when: σ² large, little variation in xⱼ, or xⱼ highly correlated with other regressors
VIF_j = 1/(1 - Rⱼ²). Rule of thumb: VIF≈1 fine, 5-10 moderate concern, >10 serious concern

Front: What does heteroskedasticity do to OLS?
Back: Does NOT bias coefficients (A4 still holds). Invalidates standard errors → wrong t-stats and p-values. Fix: heteroskedasticity-robust SE
1. Estimate model, get residuals û². 2. Regress û² on regressors. 3. LM = nR². Under H₀ (homoskedasticity): LM ~ χ²_k. Reject if p-value small

Front: Including irrelevant variables — effect on OLS?
Back: Does NOT bias other coefficients. But reduces precision: inflates standard errors

Front: Omitted variable bias — when does it occur?
Back: When you omit a variable that: (1) affects y, AND (2) is correlated with included regressors. Violates A4
Bias direction = sign(β_omitted) × sign(corr(x_omitted, x_included))
Does NOT bias coefficients. Inflates standard errors → wider CIs → harder to find significance. Extreme case: perfect multicollinearity → OLS breaks

Front: Multicollinearity vs Omitted Variable Bias — key difference?
Back: Multicollinearity: both variables included, SE inflated, no bias. OVB: variable excluded, coefficients biased. Removing a variable to fix multicollinearity can cause OVB — cure worse than disease

Front: What is a Type I error?
Back: Rejecting H₀ when it is actually true. Probability = significance level α

Front: What is a Type II error?
Back: Failing to reject H₀ when it is actually false
t = (β̂ⱼ - aⱼ) / se(β̂ⱼ). For H₀: βⱼ = 0, simplifies to t = β̂ⱼ / se(β̂ⱼ)
Left-tailed: t_obs < -t_crit. Right-tailed: t_obs > t_crit. Two-tailed: |t_obs| > t_crit

Front: Degrees of freedom for t-test in regression
Back: df = n - k - 1, where n = sample size, k = number of regressors
Probability of observing a test statistic as extreme or more extreme than what was observed, assuming H₀ is true. Reject H₀ if p-value < α

Front: t-test vs F-test — when to use each?
Back: t-test: single coefficient restriction. F-test: one OR more simultaneous restrictions. t² = F when testing single restriction — t-test is a special case of F-test
F = [(SSR_r - SSR_ur)/q] / [SSR_ur/(n-k-1)]. q = number of restrictions. Under H₀: F ~ F(q, n-k-1)
A 95% CI means: if we repeated sampling many times, 95% of constructed intervals would contain the true parameter β

Front: Dummy variable trap — what is it and how to fix?
Back: Including dummies for ALL categories creates perfect multicollinearity (they sum to 1 = intercept). Fix: drop one category — it becomes the base group

Front: Base group in dummy variable regression
Back: The omitted category. All dummy coefficients are interpreted relative to the base group, ceteris paribus
Average difference in y between group D and the base group, holding all other variables constant

Front: Perfect multicollinearity from a linear constraint — example and fix
Back: If X1+X2+X3+X4=constant, one variable is exact linear combination of others. OLS breaks. Fix: drop one variable. Its coefficient is then absorbed into the intercept, and remaining coefficients are interpreted relative to the dropped variable

Front: Why is LPM (Linear Probability Model) problematic for binary outcomes?
Back: 1. Predicted probabilities can fall outside [0,1]. 2. Error term is heteroskedastic by construction. OLS is still unbiased but inference is problematic

Front: Logit model — what function does it use?
Back: G(z) = exp(z)/(1+exp(z)) — the logistic function. Ensures P(y=1|x) ∈ (0,1)

Front: Probit model — what function does it use?
Back: G(z) = Φ(z) — the standard normal CDF. Also ensures P(y=1|x) ∈ (0,1)

Front: Why can't you interpret logit/probit coefficients directly as probability changes?
Back: Because G(·) is nonlinear. The effect of xⱼ on P(y=1) depends on the current values of all x. Use marginal effects or predicted probabilities instead
Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE), not OLS. MLE maximizes the log-likelihood: L(β) = Σ[yᵢlog G(xᵢβ) + (1-yᵢ)log(1-G(xᵢβ))]
Consistent, asymptotically normal, asymptotically efficient
LR = 2(L_ur - L_r) ~ χ²_q under H₀. Used to test multiple restrictions in logit/probit. q = number of restrictions
AUC=0.5: model no better than random guessing. AUC=1.0: perfect discrimination. Higher AUC = better ability to rank positives above negatives
TPR = TP/(TP+FN). Among actual positives, how many did the model correctly identify?
TN/(TN+FP). Among actual negatives, how many did the model correctly identify?
TP/(TP+FP). Among predicted positives, how many are actually positive?
F1 = 2 × (Precision × Recall)/(Precision + Recall). Harmonic mean of precision and recall. Useful for imbalanced classes

Front: ROC curve — what does it plot?
Back: TPR (sensitivity) on y-axis vs FPR (1-specificity) on x-axis, across all classification thresholds
Lower threshold → higher recall, lower precision. Higher threshold → lower recall, higher precision. Right balance depends on cost structure

Front: When to use PR curve over ROC?
Back: When positive class is rare (imbalanced data). ROC can be misleadingly optimistic; PR curve better reflects minority class detection
1 - L_ur/L₀, where L₀ is intercept-only log-likelihood. NOT the same as OLS R². Interpret cautiously — just a relative fit measure
AIC = -2L̂ + 2k. Lower is better. Penalizes model complexity. Use for model comparison, not absolute fit
Corr(uᵢ, uⱼ) ≠ 0 for i ≠ j. Error terms across observations predict each other. Common in time series. Violates A2

Front: No serial correlation assumption — formal statement
Back: Corr(uᵢ, uⱼ) = 0 for ALL i ≠ j. One observation's error should not predict another's
